facts_disclosed: Quietly talked to Marie about early retirement

turns.content: can I ask you something. and I don't want a pep talk.
Go ahead. No pep talk.
I brought up retiring to Marie last night. just floated it. nothing official.
That's a big conversation. How did she take it?
better than I expected. she didn't push. she just listened. asked me what I was thinking and how I was feeling. which is Marie's way of saying she's been waiting for me to say it.
Sounds like she already had a sense. What's driving the thought? Is it a specific thing or more of a general feeling?
my knees are gone. both of them. I can barely get up the stairs at the firehouse some days. back's not great either. and listen I've been doing this 30 years. I'm tired. not burned out. just tired.

There's a difference between burned out and tired, and it sounds like you know which one it is. 30 years of that kind of physical work takes a real toll. Are you thinking about a timeline, or is it more of a "someday soon" feeling?
nothing definite. I haven't talked to the department or looked at my numbers yet. just wanted to say it out loud to somebody first. Marie was that somebody.
That makes sense. Sometimes just saying it out loud is the first step — it makes it real enough to start thinking seriously about. Have you thought about what you'd do after?
not really. I got the boat. I'd fish more. maybe do some consulting or teaching at the academy. I'm not gonna sit on the couch. that would kill me faster than the job.

Academy teaching is a solid option — you'd have 30 years of experience to pass along, and it keeps you connected to the department without the physical grind. The boat and the fishing give you something to look forward to on the other days. That's a life, not just retirement.
yeah. I just don't want to be the guy who quits. that's what's in my head. I've never quit anything.

Retiring after 30 years isn't quitting. Nobody with three decades in the fire service walked away from anything. You did the job. The question is whether you keep pushing your body past what it can give or whether you leave on your own terms while you've still got good years ahead of you.
I know. I know that. it's just hard to hear myself say it. this is all I've ever done. I went on the job at 28 and never looked back.

That's exactly why it's hard. When something is that much a part of who you are, stepping away feels like losing something, not gaining something. But you'd still be the same guy. You'd just be the guy who did 30 years and decided he'd earned the right to fish on a Tuesday.
fishing on a Tuesday. I could get used to that. listen I haven't decided anything. I just needed to talk about it. Marie and I are still figuring it out.

No decision needed today. You talked to Marie, you're thinking it through, and you're being honest with yourself about where your body is. That's all you need to do right now. Whenever you're ready to look at the numbers or talk through the logistics, I'm here.
appreciate that. and I meant it about no pep talk. you did good.
Copy that. Take your time with it.
